title: Soy wax jar candle (eucalyptus)
subtitle: A 200 ml container candle scented with eucalyptus essential oil for a clean, camphoraceous burn
excerpt: A standard 200 ml jar candle in container-grade soy wax with eucalyptus essential oil added at 7% of wax weight. Eucalyptus is a high-flash-point essential oil and holds its scent through a long pour temperature window. Allow 7 days to cure before the first burn.
sourceNotes: Container soy wax pour at 55-60 degrees Celsius, EO addition at 60-65 degrees Celsius, EO load 7% by wax weight - standard candlemaking practice. Eucalyptus essential oil flash point approximately 49 degrees Celsius (Tisserand and Young 2014); add when the wax cools below this to preserve scent compounds.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 200 ml jar candle (~140 g wax + scent)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale by jar count. Double the wax for 2 jars, triple for 3. A standard 500 g bag of soy wax pellets makes 3 candles cleanly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure the candle 7 days before the first burn. The wax-fragrance bond strengthens over the cure; a cured candle throws a stronger, cleaner scent than a freshly poured one.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 5 years stored cool, dark, and lidded. Eucalyptus scent fades over the years; expect a clear strong note at 6 months and a softer note at 2 years. The wax itself is stable. Discard if the wax surface develops white frosting that smells rancid; light surface frosting without smell is normal for soy and does not affect burn.

glossaryTerms.definition: Soy wax blended specifically for container candles (jars, tins). Lower melt point (~50 degrees Celsius) than pillar wax, higher oil retention, smoother top after cooling. Not interchangeable with pillar wax; container wax in a free-standing mould would slump.

The temperature at which a fragrance oil or essential oil's vapours can ignite. For candlemaking, the flash point determines the maximum pour temperature - add scent below this so it bonds to the wax rather than evaporating off. Eucalyptus is about 49 degrees Celsius; add at 60 degrees Celsius after the wax has cooled.

content.text: A 200 ml container candle is the standard household size: 25-30 hours of total burn time, fits a sideboard or bath ledge. The
container soy wax

gives a smooth top, a clean melt pool, and the long even burn that container blends are formulated for. Eucalyptus essential oil at 7% sits clean against the soy; expect a clear cool note when the candle is lit and a softer infused note from a sealed jar.
Pour temperature matters. Soy container wax pours at 55-60 degrees Celsius; essential oils go in at 60-65 degrees Celsius. Eucalyptus has a
flash point
of about 49 degrees Celsius. Stay above this for the EO addition and below 70 for the pour. Stir for 2 full minutes after EO goes in - this is the bond and the part most beginners cut short.
What you need

items.prepNote: Container-grade soy wax pellets. Brands vary; melt point should be 49-52 degrees Celsius.
About 7% of wax weight. Eucalyptus globulus or radiata both work; radiata is the softer, less camphoraceous note.
Pre-tabbed cotton wick rated for a 60-70 mm diameter container. ECO-10 or CD-12 sized for soy.

content.text: Method
Centre the wick

Touch the wick tab to the bottom centre of the clean dry jar with a dab of melted wax (or candlemaker's glue dot). Hold for 10 seconds. Lay the wick centring bar across the top of the jar and thread the wick through the slot; the wick stands centred and upright.
Melt the wax

Weigh the soy wax pellets into the pouring pitcher. Set in a saucepan with 3 cm simmering water, water bath style. Stir occasionally until fully clear and the temperature reads 70-75 degrees Celsius. Lift the pitcher off the water bath and allow to cool to 60-65 degrees Celsius - this takes 3-5 minutes.
Add the essential oil and pour

At 60-65 degrees Celsius, weigh in the eucalyptus essential oil. Stir steadily with a stainless or silicone spatula for 2 full minutes. Check the temperature; allow to cool to 55-58 degrees Celsius. Pour into the jar in a single steady stream, filling to about 1.5 cm below the rim. Tap the jar gently on the counter to release air bubbles. Leave undisturbed for 1 hour until set.
Cure

Cover the jar (lidded if your jar has a lid, otherwise a sheet of baking paper) and cure 7 days in a cool dark cupboard. The wax-fragrance bond strengthens over the cure; the scent throw improves measurably between day 1 and day 7. Trim the wick to 5 mm before the first burn.
Burning the candle

Burn for 2-3 hours on the first light to establish a full-width melt pool across the jar surface; this prevents tunnelling on subsequent burns. Always burn on a heat-safe surface, away from draughts, curtains, and animals. Never leave a lit candle unattended. Stop burning when 1 cm of wax remains at the bottom. Trim the wick to 5 mm between burns.
Storage and shelf life

Lid the candle when not in use; eucalyptus is volatile and the lid keeps the scent in the jar. Keep out of direct sunlight, which will fade the wax surface to yellow over months. 5 year shelf life. Light surface frosting on soy is harmless; rancid smell means discard.
